channel.title: Devops on BenzHub
channel.description: Recent content in Devops on BenzHub

item.title: What Is a Cron Job in Linux? Complete Guide to Scheduled Tasks
item.description:
A cron job is a time-based task scheduler built into Linux and Unix systems. It automatically runs commands or scripts at specified intervals — every minute, hourly, daily, weekly, or on custom schedules. System administrators use cron jobs to automate backups, rotate logs, monitor services, and send scheduled reports without manual intervention.

In Linux, a cron job is the core tool for automating scheduled tasks. Once configured, the system automatically executes backups, log cleanup, service monitoring, and other repetitive tasks at specified times — greatly improving operational efficiency. This guide covers everything from cron fundamentals, time format syntax, and practical examples to debugging tips.

item.title: IP Address, Subnet Mask, Gateway & MAC Address Explained
item.description: Every device on a network needs four things configured correctly to communicate: an IP address to identify itself, a subnet mask to determine which devices are local, a gateway to reach remote networks, and a MAC address for physical delivery on the local segment. Understanding how these four networking fundamentals work together is the key to troubleshooting connectivity issues, configuring static IPs, and grasping how packets traverse the internet.

item.title: Git Staged vs Unstaged: Understanding the Staging Area
item.description: In Git, staged changes are modifications you have explicitly marked (via git add) to be included in your next commit, while unstaged changes are edits sitting in your working directory that Git tracks but will not commit until you stage them. Understanding the difference between git staged and git unstaged files is fundamental to controlling your version history and collaborating effectively with others.
